text: The surgeon begins the procedure by making an incision around the deep ring to dissociate the distal sac from the peritoneum. Dense adhesions are observed on the medial aspect of the deep ring. A standard flap development is performed, navigating through the parietal and visceral compartments, with dissection progressing towards the deep ring and the intentional peritoneal hole.

The surgeon reflects the structures laterally to the side wall, establishing a critical view of the myopectineal orifice. Femoral lymphatic tissue is visible, and the vasovasorum of the vas deferens, obliterated umbilical artery, and medial ligament are identified. The surgical field is prepared for mesh placement.

The surgeon works around the obturator foramen, parietalizing the vasculature and developing a lateral pocket. The iliopsoas muscle is identified, and the chord elements, including the vas deferens, are parietalized. The prominent prostate is visible. The transversalis sling is used to evaluate and identify possible cord lipomas, differentiating them from iliac lymph and fat, which penetrate posterior to the sling.

The surgeon identifies a small ribbon of fat overlying the posterior transversalis sling and carefully explores it to confirm the presence of a distal lipoma. The areolar tissues are adhesed to the distal sac, which retracts back upon reduction. The structures are identified, and a solid critical view is developed, preparing for mesh positioning. An extra-large mid-weight mesh is positioned and secured laterally near the most prominent defect, followed by a standard flap closure.

The surgeon closes the flap from lateral to medial, addressing the visible fenestration created during preperitoneal dissection. The tissue edges come together smoothly, completing the standard closure.
